perturbation.original_text: 12.4. HealthGate will perform the Services and meet the Specifications and Service Levels set forth and referred to in this Agreement. In all cases where HealthGate has not committed to a specific performance standard, HealthGate will use reasonable care in providing the Services.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.4. HealthGate will perform the Services and meet the Specifications and Service Levels set forth and referred to in this Agreement. In all cases where HealthGate has not committed to a specific performance standard, HealthGate will use best efforts in providing the Services.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable care' to 'best efforts' reduces the standard of care HealthGate is legally required to provide. This omission creates a legal contradiction since it weakens the consumer protection for adequate service.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2, prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. By changing the standard of care from "reasonable care" to "best efforts," HealthGate reduces its obligation to provide adequate service, which can be considered an unfair practice, particularly if it leads to substandard service delivery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The change from "reasonable care" to "best efforts" weakens the consumer protection standard required, creating a legal contradiction. M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2(a), prohibits "unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce." The reduction in the standard of care could be interpreted as an unfair practice, as it potentially allows for substandard service delivery, violating the intent of the law as described in Snippet 1: "Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful."

perturbation.original_text: HealthGate will ensure that all documents, data and Software are kept under secure conditions with back up arrangements satisfactory to the Publishers, to protect them effectively from unauthorised access and so that they can be recovered from any malfunction of the System.
perturbation.changed_text: HealthGate will ensure that all documents, data and Software are kept under secure conditions to protect them from unauthorised access.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'back up arrangements satisfactory to the Publishers, so that they can be recovered from any malfunction of the System' reduces the data protection measures and creates a legal contradiction since it violates consumer data protection laws by not ensuring data recovery capabilities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Data Security Law - M.G.L. c. 93H
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93H requires businesses to implement and maintain a comprehensive information security program to protect personal information, which includes ensuring the secure backup and recovery of data. Removing the assurance of satisfactory backup arrangements directly contradicts this requirement, increasing the risk of data loss and violating consumer data protection laws.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'back up arrangements satisfactory to the Publishers, so that they can be recovered from any malfunction of the System' directly contradicts the consumer data protection law because it doesn't ensure data recovery capabilities, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The changed text weakens the data protection measures. The remaining snippet, 'Chapter 93H: SECURITY BREACHES,' indicates the importance of security and data protection, aligning with the need for data recovery arrangements. The original text provided a higher standard of data security, while the changed text reduces this standard, creating a legally relevant omission.

perturbation.original_text: HealthGate shall be solely responsible for the compliance by its personnel with all laws and regulations of any pertinent countries relating to data protection and privacy and/or transborder data flow.
perturbation.changed_text: HealthGate shall be responsible for the compliance by its personnel with some laws and regulations of any pertinent countries relating to data protection and privacy and/or transborder data flow.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all' to 'some' significantly weakens the data protection commitment, creating a legal loophole. This omission creates a legal contradiction since it violates GDPR and other international data protection laws that require full compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 5 mandates that personal data shall be processed lawfully, fairly, and transparently. By limiting HealthGate's responsibility to only *some* data protection laws, the contract opens the door for non-compliance with other relevant regulations, violating the fundamental principle of lawful and fair data processing under GDPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: f of the European Commission is responsible for the use which might be made of the following information. As this guidance reflects the state of the art at the time of its drafting, it should be regarded as a 'living tool' open for improvement and its content may be subject to modifications without notice. International dimension of data protection The EU has established international data protection agreements to ensure that EU citizens' personal data remains protected even if transferred outside the EU. EU data protection legislation includes safeguards for when transferring data to third countries, including adequacy decisions, standard contractual clauses (SCC) and binding corporate rules (BCR). Find out more about the international dimension of data protection Funding to support the implementation of the GDPR The Commission has provided funding to national data protection authorities to finance projects that support the implementation of the GDPR. These projects aim to equip individuals and businesses with the knowledge and resources needed to navigate and ensure compliance with data protection rules. The types of projects funded by the Commission typically include awareness-raising campaigns, training programs, and the development of practical tools and materials that can facilitate small and medium-sized enterprises' (SMEs) compliance with the GDPR. Find out more about these EU-funded projects Simplifying compliance with the GDPR through reduced record-keeping obligations In May 2025, the Commission adopted a Single Market Simplification proposal to cut €300 million in annual administrative cost for companies. In order to respond to calls from Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) and Small and Mid-Caps companies (SMCs), the Commission introduced targeted changes to the rules on record-keeping for small and medium-sized companies and organisations under 750 employees. They include namely: Extending the derogation from record-keeping obligation under Article 30(5) of the GDPR to SMCs and organisations under 750 employees. Requiring SMEs, SMCs and organisations with fewer than 750 employees to maintain records only when their processing of personal data can be considered “high risk” under the GDPR. The proposal ensures to maintain robust standards of data protection and respects the GDPR risk-based approach. It does not affect the rest of the provisions of the GDPR. Timeline 21 May 2025 Commission proposes targeted changes to record-keeping obligations. 25 July 2024 The Commission publishes the second report on the application of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). 4 July 2023 The Commission proposes further specifying procedural rules relating to the enforcement of the GDPR. 14 October 2022 The Commission publishes the first report on the application of the Data Protection Regulation for EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies (EUDPR). 25 July 2022 The Commission publishes the first report on the application of the LED. 24 June 2020 The Commission publishes the first report on the application of the GDPR. 11 December 2018 The EUDPR becomes applicable. 23 October 2018 The European Parliament and the Council adopt the EUDPR. 25 May 2018 The GDPR becomes applicable. 6 May 2018 The Member States have to transpose the LED into their national law. 10 January 2017 The Commission proposes the EUDPR . 24 May 2016 The GDPR enters into force. 5 May 2016 The LED enters into force. 27 April 2016 The European Parliament and the Council adopt the LED and the GDPR. 25 January 2012 The European Commission proposes a comprehensive reform of the EU's 1995 data protection rules to strengthen online privacy rights and boost Europe's digital economy. 4 November 2010 The Commission presents a Communication to the other EU institutions on "A comprehensive approach on personal data protection in EU". perturbation.justification: The change from 'all' to 'some' weakens the commitment to data protection laws. The Perturbation Explanation indicates a significant weakening of the data protection commitment, creating a legal loophole violating GDPR and international data protection laws. The Law Explanation states that GDPR Article 5 mandates lawful, fair, and transparent processing of personal data. By limiting HealthGate's responsibility to 'some' data protection laws, the contract creates a clear path for non-compliance and thus violates the principle of lawful and fair data processing under GDPR. While the provided scraped law snippet does not directly address GDPR article 5, it generally discusses the EU's data protection rules and international data protection agreements, which supports the notion that comprehensive data protection compliance is expected, not partial compliance.
